critic_raw:
The methodology is well-constructed overall, but I see several genuine issues worth raising.

**Issue 1: Repository stratification and temporal stratification are in tension — the design may be under-specified in a way that creates leakage or renders the temporal control ineffective.**

The claim is that the combined temporal+repository split prevents both leakage types simultaneously. The mechanism of failure: if 70% of repositories appear only in training, and those repositories are active throughout years 1–5, then the training set necessarily contains data from years 3.5–5 (from training-only repos). The design says 'all PRs from the same repository appear in exactly one split' — but that directly contradicts having a temporal split. You cannot simultaneously say (a) all PRs from repo X appear only in training, and (b) training covers only years 1–3.5, if repo X has PRs in years 4–5. The resolution must be one of: (i) repository assignment to splits ignores the temporal range, (ii) the temporal split is enforced only within shared repositories, or (iii) only repos that existed and were active only in the training period are in the training set. Option (i) means training data contains future PRs from training-only repos, introducing temporal leakage for those repos. Option (iii) creates systematic selection bias (older, long-lived repos dominate training). The design does not specify which resolution is intended, and the wrong choice silently invalidates both controls. Evidence: examine the actual time ranges of PRs in each split — if training-set PRs include post-cutoff dates from training-only repos, leakage has occurred.

**Issue 2: The text embedding model is described as 'fit on training-set messages only' but this is conflated with the embedding model itself being pre-trained on external corpora.**

The claim is that fitting on training data only prevents leakage from text embeddings. The mechanism of failure: a pre-trained or lightly fine-tuned model (e.g., BERT) encodes knowledge from external corpora that may include post-training-period technical documents, code discussions, or even content from the evaluated repositories if they are open-source or if their commit messages appear in training corpora for foundation models. The 'fit on training set' language applies to scalers and task-specific heads, but the base embedding weights carry external information whose temporal boundary is undefined. This is a known problem in NLP-assisted temporal evaluation. Evidence: ablate with a randomly initialized embedding model (or one with a confirmed pre-training cutoff before year 1 of the experiment). If performance drops significantly, the pre-trained weights are contributing signal beyond what the described leakage controls address. Alternatively, document the pre-training cutoff of the embedding model and verify it precedes the training period.

**Issue 3: The repository stratification design makes the test-set class imbalance unknowable in advance, which may cause AUPRC to be dominated by a small number of repositories with atypically high defect rates.**

The claim is that AUPRC is preferable to threshold-specific metrics because defect prevalence varies by repo and time. But this is also its failure mode: if the 15% of test repositories happen (by the random stratification draw) to have a very different base defect rate from training repositories, the AUPRC value becomes hard to interpret and may not reflect deployment utility. Specifically, repositories with high defect rates trivially boost AUPRC even for weak models. The per-repository precision secondary metric partially addresses this, but it is secondary. Evidence: report AUPRC stratified by repository defect rate quartile. If AUPRC is materially higher in high-defect-rate test repos, the primary metric is partially a function of the stratification draw rather than model quality. This could be controlled by stratifying the repository assignment on defect rate when constructing splits.

**Issue 4: The baseline is not a genuine ablation of the text contribution because the baseline includes author history (prior PR defect rate), which is a weak proxy for the same signal the text model is supposed to provide.**

The claim is that the comparison isolates the contribution of text and fine-grained code statistics. The mechanism of failure: author defect history is a strong signal that may account for a large fraction of the defect prediction variance. If the text model also learns author-correlated patterns (specific commit message styles per author), then the incremental AUPRC of adding text embeddings is suppressed relative to an 'information-equivalent' baseline without author history. Conversely, if author history is removed from the baseline, the baseline becomes artificially weak. The current design means the marginal value of text is evaluated against a baseline that already has a strong author-level signal — this is the right comparison for practical deployment, but it does not cleanly isolate the text contribution. This is a framing issue rather than a validity issue, but it means the claim 'text embeddings add value' could be true while simultaneously 'text embeddings add no value beyond author history' is also true. Evidence: add a third model — proposed features minus text embeddings plus author history — to directly decompose contributions.

**Summary:** Issue 1 is the most fundamental because it concerns whether the leakage controls actually operate as described. Issues 2 and 3 are genuine methodological concerns. Issue 4 is a limitation of the ablation design that weakens interpretability of results but does not invalidate the main claim.
all_issues_raised: Repository stratification and temporal stratification are in direct logical tension: assigning all PRs from a repo to a single split while also enforcing temporal cutoffs is contradictory if repos span multiple time periods, creating either temporal leakage or systematic selection bias depending on which constraint is enforced.

The pre-trained embedding model may carry external corpus knowledge with an undefined temporal boundary, meaning 'fit on training set only' does not actually bound the information available to the text features.

AUPRC computed over a small, randomly stratified set of held-out repositories may be dominated by repos with atypically high defect rates, making the primary metric sensitive to the stratification draw rather than model quality.

The baseline includes author defect history, a proxy for the same variance the text model captures, so the ablation does not cleanly isolate the text contribution and a finer decomposition is needed to support mechanistic claims.

The temporal split and repository stratification constraints are mutually under-specified and may be silently violated in implementation, conflating time and repository as confounds rather than controlling them independently.

Commit message embeddings may capture organization-wide label-correlated lexical patterns (e.g., 'hotfix:', revert language) rather than semantic content predictive of defects, and the experiment lacks an ablation distinguishing pre-trained semantic representations from lexical pattern matching.

AUPRC as primary metric is sensitive to class prevalence variation across repositories and time periods; if test-set prevalence differs from training, absolute AUPRC is misleading and relative comparisons may be distorted, and the aggregation method (micro vs. macro) is unspecified.

PRs merged within 180 days of the dataset collection cutoff have truncated label observation windows and will be systematically mislabeled as clean, creating a recency-correlated false-negative artifact in the test set.
PRs merged near the data cutoff have truncated 180-day label observation windows, creating systematic under-labeling in the test set that inflates apparent model performance.

The interaction between temporal split and repository-level stratification is underspecified: it is ambiguous whether training-period PRs from test-designated repositories are included in training or discarded, with material consequences for training data diversity.

Pre-trained or fine-tuned embedding models may encode distributional knowledge of commit message conventions from data postdating the training cutoff, partially circumventing the temporal leakage controls.

AUPRC is sensitive to class prevalence, and disjoint test repositories may have different defect rates than training repositories, making cross-cohort AUPRC comparisons unreliable for the same reason the methodology rejects threshold-specific metrics.

Text embeddings and code-change statistics are bundled as a single feature set with no ablation, so the experiment cannot confirm whether text specifically contributes signal or whether code-change statistics alone account for the gain over baseline.

assessor_results.critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experiment design with thoughtful attention to leakage and confounding. However, there are several genuine methodological concerns, grouped by root cause.

---

**Group 1: The temporal split and repository stratification are partially in conflict, creating a silent misconfiguration risk**

The design calls for 70% of repositories to appear only in training, 15% only in validation, 15% only in test — AND a temporal split where training covers years 1–3.5, validation 3.5–4.5, test 4.5–5+. These two constraints are jointly applied, but the description says 'all PRs from the same repository appear in exactly one split.' This means a repository whose activity spans all 5 years is nonetheless assigned entirely to one partition. If a high-volume repository is assigned to test (covering years 4.5–5+), its training-period PRs (years 1–3.5) are discarded. Conversely, a repository assigned to training contributes no evaluation signal even if it produced defect-prone PRs in year 5. The result is that the effective training and test set compositions depend heavily on repository assignment choices that are not fully specified — and naive implementation could violate either the temporal or the repository constraint depending on which is prioritized. The design needs to explicitly state whether the repository split is stratified by temporal activity (e.g., only repos active during the test window qualify for test assignment), and whether repos spanning multiple periods are split or discarded. Without this, the experiment could be silently misconfigured: 'cross-repository generalization' might actually be tested on repositories with no temporal overlap with training, conflating time and repository as confounds rather than controlling them separately.

Evidence: A distribution table of repository assignment by activity period would reveal whether the temporal and repository constraints are actually simultaneously satisfied.

---

**Group 2: The text embedding's signal may be proxying label-correlated metadata rather than commit-message semantics**

The proposed model encodes commit messages via a pre-trained dense embedding. The claim is that this adds signal beyond numeric features. However, commit messages in enterprise repositories often contain structured tokens: ticket IDs ('JIRA-1234'), project prefixes, conventional prefixes ('fix:', 'hotfix:', 'revert:'), and team-specific idioms. These patterns may directly correlate with the label — e.g., commits already tagged 'fix' at write time will have high embedding similarity to labeled defect-fix commits, but this is not a predictive signal; it is a near-circular dependency. The train/test repo split does reduce the risk of repo-specific idiom memorization, but 'hotfix:' and revert-adjacent language are organization-wide patterns that would generalize across repositories and still be informative for labeling reasons rather than causal defect-prediction reasons.

More specifically: if the label generation (step 1) links PRs to 'bug-fix commits via issue tracker,' the commit messages of subsequent fix commits are NOT in the feature set. But the original PR's commit messages may contain forward-looking notes ('workaround for X, needs proper fix'), reviewer comments flagging concerns, or developer hedging language. These are legitimately useful semantic signals — but they're also correlated with review process (a baseline feature). The experiment doesn't establish whether embedding gains are orthogonal to or redundant with review metadata. The baseline includes review duration and reviewer count but not review comment text, so it's possible the embedding is partially capturing review sentiment that is simply missing from the baseline feature set rather than capturing something in commit messages per se.

Evidence: An ablation encoding review comment text as a bag-of-words or TF-IDF vs. commit message embeddings would reveal whether the gain is from 'text in general' or 'pre-trained semantic representations of commit content specifically.' If TF-IDF on commit messages matches BERT, the pre-trained model adds nothing over lexical pattern matching.

---

**Group 3: AUPRC as primary metric may favor the proposed model for distributional reasons unrelated to ranking quality**

AUPRC is sensitive to class imbalance and, critically, to the prevalence of positives in the evaluation set. The design notes that 'defect prevalence can vary by repository and time period' as motivation for AUPRC over threshold-specific metrics — but this variation is precisely what makes AUPRC comparisons across experimental conditions unreliable. If the test repositories (15% of repos, assigned non-randomly since temporal activity constrains which repos qualify) happen to have higher defect rates than training repositories, AUPRC will be inflated for the test set relative to a deployment scenario where defect rates match training-set prevalence. Conversely, if test repos are newer (more likely if active in years 4.5–5+) and newer repos tend toward lower defect rates (less accumulated bug history), AUPRC will be deflated. Either scenario makes the absolute AUPRC number hard to interpret, and the relative comparison between proposed and baseline could be affected if the two models have different score distributions that interact differently with changed prevalence.

The design uses 'per-repository precision' as a secondary metric but doesn't specify whether AUPRC is computed globally or per-repository-then-averaged. Global AUPRC is dominated by high-volume repositories; per-repo AUPRC is noisier for small repos. This choice should be pre-specified.

Evidence: Report AUPRC alongside observed positive rate in each split. Report both micro-averaged and macro-averaged AUPRC. If the two diverge substantially, prevalence variation is distorting the primary metric.

---

**Group 4: The 180-day label window interacts with the temporal split in a way that may silently truncate test labels**

The training cutoff is year 3.5. Labels require observing 180 days post-merge. For PRs merged near the end of the training window (year 3.5), labels are generated from events up to approximately year 4 — which falls inside the validation period. This is acknowledged as frozen, which is fine. But for the test set (PRs merged in years 4.5–5+), PRs merged in the final ~180 days of the observation window (the last 6 months of year 5+) may have truncated label observation if the dataset collection ends at year 5. A PR merged in month 58 of a 60-month dataset has only 2 months of post-merge observation, not 6. These PRs would be labeled 'clean' not because they are clean but because the follow-up window is incomplete. If the experiment doesn't censor or exclude these PRs, the test set contains a systematically mislabeled tail, biasing both models toward predicting 'clean' on recent PRs and potentially creating a recency-correlated accuracy artifact.

The design says the 180-day window is 'frozen before the experiment begins' but does not say late-window PRs are excluded or censored. This is a concrete labeling error that would be invisible in aggregate metrics but would depress recall on the most recent PRs.

Evidence: Plot defect rate as a function of merge date in the test set. A declining defect rate in the final 6 months of the test set (relative to earlier months) is a strong indicator of truncated label observation. Excluding PRs without a full 180-day observation window post-merge should be pre-specified.

---

**Summary**

The most critical issues are: (1) the conflict between temporal and repository split constraints that could produce a silently misconfigured evaluation, and (4) the label truncation at the end of the observation window. Issue (2) regarding text signal confounding is a genuine concern but addressable with ablations. Issue (3) regarding AUPRC instability under prevalence variation is a design choice that needs pre-specification to be interpretable. None of these are fatal to the overall approach, but issues (1) and (4) could invalidate the reported test results without any visible warning signal.

issues_raised: PRs merged near the data cutoff have truncated 180-day label observation windows, creating systematic under-labeling in the test set that inflates apparent model performance.

The interaction between temporal split and repository-level stratification is underspecified: it is ambiguous whether training-period PRs from test-designated repositories are included in training or discarded, with material consequences for training data diversity.

Pre-trained or fine-tuned embedding models may encode distributional knowledge of commit message conventions from data postdating the training cutoff, partially circumventing the temporal leakage controls.

AUPRC is sensitive to class prevalence, and disjoint test repositories may have different defect rates than training repositories, making cross-cohort AUPRC comparisons unreliable for the same reason the methodology rejects threshold-specific metrics.

Text embeddings and code-change statistics are bundled as a single feature set with no ablation, so the experiment cannot confirm whether text specifically contributes signal or whether code-change statistics alone account for the gain over baseline.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
The methodology is carefully designed and addresses several common pitfalls explicitly. I'll evaluate it systematically for genuine structural issues rather than surface-level concerns.

**Issue 1: Repository stratification creates a latent confound with temporal distribution**

The claim: partitioning 70/15/15% of repositories into train/val/test ensures cross-repository generalization without leakage.

The mechanism of failure: Repository age is not uniformly distributed across the 5-year window. Older repositories disproportionately appear in early years; newer repositories in later years. When you assign repositories entirely to one split, you're not just stratifying by repository identity — you're also stratifying by repository maturity, programming language era, and historical defect rate. Test repositories will tend to be younger (or at least have shorter post-merge observation windows), and the 180-day label window may be systematically less complete for recent PRs. A PR merged in year 4.8 has only ~60 days of actual observation before the 5-year data cutoff, yet still requires 180 days for labeling. This is not explicitly addressed.

Evidence for/against: Compute the empirical label rate as a function of time-to-data-cutoff. If PRs near the cutoff have materially lower defect-prone label rates (because the observation window is truncated), this is label truncation bias that will cause the model to appear better than it is on recent PRs — precisely the test set.

**Issue 2: The repository stratification interacts with the temporal split in an underspecified way**

The claim: temporal split and repository stratification are applied jointly such that test repos appear only in years 4.5–5+ and are disjoint from training repos.

The mechanism of failure: The design says 'all PRs from the same repository appear in exactly one split.' This means the 15% of test repositories contribute PRs across all time periods — but only the time-period portion falling in years 4.5–5+ is used (since that's the test window). If a 'test repository' was active in years 1–3 and the methodology means those early PRs are excluded from training, this introduces a selection bias: the training distribution lacks signal from that repository's conventions entirely. Conversely, if 'test repository' means the repository only appears in the test time window, then the stratification is not truly about generalization to unseen repositories — it's just temporal split with repository-level labeling. The interaction between these two split axes needs to be made explicit; the current design is ambiguous about which repository-PRs are dropped and why.

Evidence for/against: State explicitly how many training PRs are discarded due to belonging to a 'test repository' that was active in the training period. If this number is large, the design wastes data and creates a training distribution that does not reflect the true historical diversity.

**Issue 3: Text embedding fit point creates an implicit distribution mismatch**

The claim: 'The embedding model is fit on training-set messages only' — this is described as the pre-leakage safeguard for the text branch.

The mechanism of failure: The methodology proposes using a 'pre-trained or lightly fine-tuned' embedding model. Pre-trained models (BERT, etc.) are trained on internet text corpora that may include public code repositories from the future relative to the training window. If the embedding model has been pre-trained on GitHub data post-year 3.5, it encodes distributional knowledge of commit message conventions, project names, and even repository-specific terminology that postdates the training cutoff. 'Lightly fine-tuned' means the backbone was not fit solely on train-set data. This is not strictly a temporal leakage issue for the gradient-boosted model downstream, but it means the embedding space is shaped by future data, which could systematically improve representation of test-set commit messages in ways that are invisible to the leakage controls described.

Evidence for/against: Compare model performance using a fully frozen, non-fine-tuned pre-trained model vs. a model fine-tuned on training-set messages only vs. a TF-IDF baseline fit solely on training data. If fine-tuning on training-only data and using a fixed pre-trained model produce similar AUPRC deltas over baseline, the concern is weak. If the fine-tuned version materially outperforms the frozen version, the fine-tuning process and its data sourcing deserve scrutiny.

**Issue 4: AUPRC as primary metric conflates ranking quality with practical utility given label distribution shift across repository cohorts**

The claim: AUPRC is appropriate because 'defect prevalence can vary by repository and time period, so a threshold-specific metric may move for reasons unrelated to model quality.'

The mechanism of failure: AUPRC is itself sensitive to class prevalence — the absolute value of AUPRC changes directly with the positive rate in the evaluation set, even for a perfectly calibrated and equally discriminative model. The argument given for preferring AUPRC over threshold-specific metrics (prevalence variation across repos) is also an argument against using AUPRC as a cross-cohort comparable metric. If test repositories have a different defect rate than training repositories (which is plausible since they are by construction disjoint), AUPRC comparisons between the proposed and baseline model are still confounded by prevalence. The secondary metric of per-repository precision partially mitigates this, but is not the primary metric and does not affect model selection.

Evidence for/against: Report AUPRC decomposed by defect prevalence quintile across repositories. If AUPRC tracks prevalence more strongly than it tracks model quality, the metric is giving the wrong signal. The normalized AUPRC (divided by the theoretical maximum given the prior) or the PR-AUC skill score would be more stable.

**Issue 5: The 'no mechanism' disclaimer doesn't protect against confounded attribution**

The claim: The experiment establishes that text embeddings + code-change statistics improve defect prediction; mechanism/interpretability is out of scope.

The mechanism of failure: This is less a methodological flaw and more a missing validity check. The text embeddings are 768-dimensional vectors from commit messages. Code-change statistics are numeric. These are bundled as a single feature set compared against a baseline that omits both. If the improvement comes entirely from file-level code statistics (e.g., total files modified, deletion ratio) and not from text embeddings, the experiment's narrative — that text provides signal — is wrong even if AUPRC improves. There is no ablation between text-only and code-stats-only within the proposed feature set.

Evidence for/against: Run three models: (a) baseline features only, (b) baseline + code-change stats, (c) baseline + code-change stats + text embeddings. If (b) vs. (a) accounts for most of the gain over baseline and (c) vs. (b) is marginal, the text embedding hypothesis is not supported by the data even if the headline comparison supports the feature set.

**Summary:**
Issues 1 and 2 (label truncation + split interaction ambiguity) are structural and could silently inflate test performance. Issue 3 (embedding pre-training provenance) is real but likely small in effect. Issue 4 (AUPRC sensitivity to prevalence) undermines the metric choice rationale. Issue 5 (missing ablation) means the central hypothesis — that text embeddings specifically help — cannot be confirmed without additional structure. Issues 1, 4, and 5 are the most actionable.
